sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Miscarriage is the spontaneous loss of a pregnancy before 20 weeks' gestation. Early pregnancy bleeding is common and does not always indicate pregnancy loss, but heavy bleeding, shock, severe pain, or uncertain diagnosis require urgent review.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Vaginal bleeding
- Lower abdominal pain or cramping
- Passage of tissue or clots
- Loss of pregnancy symptoms
- Hypotension or tachycardia if significant blood loss is present
- Fever or offensive discharge if septic miscarriage is suspected

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Early pregnancy bleeding does not always represent miscarriage
- Ectopic pregnancy must be considered in all patients with abdominal pain and bleeding in early pregnancy until excluded
- Prehospital diagnosis may remain uncertain, especially if products of conception are not obvious
- Ask about gestation, prior ultrasound confirmation of intrauterine pregnancy, amount of bleeding, passage of tissue, pain severity, syncope, and fever
- Heavy bleeding, shock, severe unilateral pain, or sepsis features should lower the threshold for urgent transport

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer support, privacy, and reassurance
- Perform a full vital sign survey and reassess frequently if bleeding or pain is changing
- Administer oxygen if hypoxaemic, shocked, or in respiratory distress
- Keep the patient warm and minimise unnecessary exertion if significant bleeding is suspected
- Maintain a low threshold for contacting the on-call doctor or RFDS
- If tissue or fetus is passed later in gestation, preserve respectfully and transport with the patient where possible

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain ECG if shock, syncope, or diagnostic uncertainty is present
- Consider IV access if significant bleeding, severe pain, or ongoing deterioration is suspected
- Consider point-of-care haemoglobin where available if bleeding is clinically significant
- Consider IV fluids if hypovolaemia or shock is suspected, while expediting transport
- Consider analgesia and antiemetic therapy in line with local protocol
- Consider antibiotics only in consultation with the on-call doctor if septic miscarriage is suspected
- Manage suspected sepsis under the sepsis guideline

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Definitive diagnosis often requires ultrasound or confirmed passage of products of conception
- Retain large clots or tissue for hospital review if possible
- Miscarriage can have a profound psychological impact and care should remain sensitive and respectful
